observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
a. reconstitute the Inventory Committee and require them to conduct complete
physical count of the Municipality’s PPE according to their prepared Physical
Inventory Plan and prepare the corresponding Report on the Physical Count of
Property, Plant and Equipment (RPCPPE) following the guidelines set forth in
COA Circular No. 2020-006;
b. direct the OIC - General Services Officer to coordinate with the Municipal
Accountant, Municipal Treasurer and Municipal Engineer in securing the
necessary documents like land titles, contracts and disbursement payments
of land acquisition and construction of infrastructure assets as basis in
recording and updating their respective PPE Property Cards (PC);
c. require the OIC - General Services Officer to update all PCs and records based
on the latest RPCPPE; and
d. require the OIC - General Services Officer and Municipal Accountant to exert
their best efforts to reconcile their respective PPE records to come up with the
reconciled balances of the PPE accounts to be presented in the financial
statements.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
a. instruct the Municipal Treasurer to prepare the RPTAR or certified list of
taxpayers and the amounts collectible from them and to submit it to the
Municipal Accountant who shall use it as her basis to set-up Real Property Tax
and Special Education Tax Receivables at the beginning of every year; and
b. require the Municipal Accountant to adjust or correct the balances of RPT
and SET Receivables, Deferred Real Property Tax and Deferred Special
Education Tax Income and the Other Deferred Credits by excluding the
uncollected Fines and Penalties.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Legal Officer
and/or other concerned Municipal Officials to:
a. initiate the termination of the subject contract in accordance with Annex I of
the 2016 Revised Implementing Rules and Regulations of Republic Act
No. 9184 and the blacklisting of the Contractor pursuant to his Performance
Securing Declaration form dated March 24, 2022;
b. demand from the Contractor the return of the balance of the advance payment
made by the Municipality amounting to P484,261.26 and to pay necessary
damages as a result of the abandonment of the project; and
c. file appropriate legal action against the Contractor and other responsible
officials and employees, if warranted.

Henceforth, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the BAC and the
Municipal Accountant to:
a. ensure the submission to and acceptance by the Municipality of an irrevocable
standby letter of credit of equivalent value from a commercial bank, a bank
guarantee or a surety bond callable upon demand, issued by a surety or
insurance company duly licensed by the Insurance Commission and
confirmed by the procuring entity, before release of advance payment to
contractor; and
b. strictly comply with pertinent provisions of the 2016 RIRR of RA 9184 on
deduction of retention money from progress billings and the conditions for
their release to the contractor.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the concerned BAC-TWG to
provide written explanation on why the involved contractor was not disqualified
despite non-submission of a valid business permit and henceforth, direct the TWG
to strictly adhere to the requirements, rules and guidelines on the post-
qualification evaluation of bidders as provided under Section 34 of the 2016 RIRR
of RA No. 9184, otherwise the relevant procurement and payments to the bidder,
who was supposedly disqualified, will be disallowed in audit, with the
participating BAC members included as liable persons.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant and
Municipal Treasurer to locate and submit immediately to the COA Audit Team the
five missing DVs with all the supporting documents and the original copies of the
photocopied DVs and supporting documents previously submitted to the Audit
Team to facilitate the audit of the transactions. Otherwise, the subject
disbursements will be disallowed in audit.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Legal Officer
and/or other concerned Municipal Officials to:
a. instruct the Municipal Engineer to determine the actual work accomplished by
the Contractor and demand from the latter the return of the overpayment and
payment of necessary damages as a result of the non-completion and
abandonment of the project; and
b. file appropriate legal action against the Contractor and other responsible
officials and employees, if warranted.

Henceforth, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the BAC and the
Municipal Accountant to:
a. ensure the posting and submission by contractors of the required
Performance Security or Performance Securing Declaration, before award of
contracts to them; and
b. strictly comply with pertinent provisions of the 2016 RIRR of RA 9184 on
deduction of retention money from progress billings and the conditions for
their release to the contractor.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant and
the BAC to:
a. submit immediately the lacking supporting documents for the procurement of
the three infrastructure projects, subject to verification of the Audit Team, to
avoid audit suspension and/or audit disallowance; and
b. strictly comply with the documentary requirements enumerated in Items 9.1
and 9.1.1 of COA Circular 2012-001 dated June 14, 2012 and Section 17.1 of the
2016 Revised Implementing Rules and Regulations (RIRR) of Republic Act (RA)
No. 9184 in the conduct of public biddings and processing of payments of
infrastructure projects.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Engineering
Office to conduct preliminary inspection and submit punch-lists to contractors on
infrastructure projects when they reach an accomplishment of 95 percent of the
total contract amount to determine defects and thus, make necessary corrections,
before proper turnover and acceptance of the projects to the Municipality.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct:
a. all concerned officials to strictly comply with the COA guidelines on proper
filling out and approval of Driver’s Trip Ticket except when the travel is proven
to be confidential in nature, in which case a certification to that effect should
be submitted;
b. the OIC – Head of the General Services Office to prepare and submit the
monthly report of fuel consumption to the COA Audit Team;
c. the Municipal Accountant to ensure completeness of driver’s trip tickets,
purchase orders and charge invoices before processing the payments for
gasoline consumptions, otherwise the transactions will be suspended or
disallowed in audit;
d. Municipal Administrator and the OIC – Head of the General Services Office to
formulate additional guidelines on the use of motor vehicles and/or revisit
existing policies of the Municipality on fuel utilization, if there is any; and
e. Municipal Administrator and other concerned officials to study the advantages
and disadvantages of sourcing the gasoline need of the Municipality thru the
use of fleet cards being provided by some gasoline stations as what other
government agencies are doing for better control and monitoring of its fuel
consumption.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
a. instruct the Municipal Treasurer and Information Technology OIC - Department
Head to immediately coordinate with the computerized system service
provider to review the system and recommend necessary upgrade or
improvement thereon that will address the issue on the generation of detailed
daily collection reports of the Municipal Treasurer’s Office Tellers; and
b. require the Municipal Treasurer and Municipal Accountant to institute
measures, controls and a review process that will give assurance that the
collections of the Tellers are recorded and classified correctly in accordance
with existing accounting rules and regulations.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the OIC - Municipal Engineer
and other concerned officials to ensure that the required project signboards
and/or public notices are displayed at the project location and the information
therein such as the start date of the construction, target date of completion, cost
incurred, percentage of completion as of given date are complete and up-to-date.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Market Administrator and
the Municipal Market Board to provide him with complete information on the
status of lease and occupancy of all market stall holders and to initiate the
process of revoking the lease agreement/certificate of award of Stall Holders who
subleased or sold the market stalls, including the 105 initially established in audit,
and cause the execution of lease agreement between the Municipal Government
and present occupants.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
a. instruct the Market Administrator to strictly enforce the provisions of the
Municipal Market Code and the contract of lease of market stalls and cause the
revocation or cancellation of lease agreement/certificate of award of
stallholders in case of failure of the delinquent stallholders to immediately
comply with the demand of the Municipal Treasurer to settle their liabilities
after observing due process of law;
b. require the Market Administrator, in coordination with the Municipal Treasurer
to submit the List/Schedule of Lessees with delinquent accounts and with
current unpaid rental fees as of December 31, 2022 to the Municipal
Accountant for recording as Accounts Receivable;
c. instruct the Municipal Accountant to prepare the necessary Journal Entry
Voucher based on the Schedule of Lessees with delinquent accounts and with
current unpaid rental fees as of December 31, 2022; and
d. instruct the Market Administrator and Municipal Treasurer to consistently send
demand/collection letters to delinquent Tenants/Lessees, and to recommend to the
Market Board the termination of contracts of those who continuously refuse to
comply with their demand for payments, otherwise Notice of Charge will be
issued by the Audit Team that will include all Municipal Officials who must but
did not exercise due diligence or take necessary action to collect the amount due
the Municipal Government.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor: a. reconstitute the Inventory Committee and require them to conduct complete physical count of the Municipality’s PPE according to their prepared Physical Inventory Plan and prepare the corresponding Report on the Physical Count of Property, Plant and Equipment (RPCPPE) following the guidelines set forth in COA Circular No. 2020-006; b. direct the OIC - General Services Officer to coordinate with the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Engineer in securing the necessary documents like land titles, contracts and disbursement payments of land acquisition and construction of infrastructure assets as basis in recording and updating their respective PPE Property Cards (PC); c. require the OIC - General Services Officer to update all PCs and records based on the latest RPCPPE; and d. require the OIC - General Services Officer and Municipal Accountant to exert their best efforts to reconcile their respective PPE records to come up with the reconciled balances of the PPE accounts to be presented in the financial statements.
observations.managementAction: Conducted
observations.reason: Only partial physical count of PPE was implemented.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant to coordinate with the Provincial Accountant to initiate the process of reconciliation of their respective Due from LGU accounts, after which make adjustments in the books of the Municipality, if necessary.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accountant provided a certification to the Provincial Accountant stating that there was no record or any document to validate the claims Due to Real Property Tax for the period 1990 – 1997 amounting P511,513.65. Also, the Province requested for write-off of the said account.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor: a. instruct the Local Disaster Risk Reduction Management Council (LDRRMC) to facilitate the preparation of the LDRRMFIP including the unexpended balances from previous years; b. direct the Municipal Planning and Development Officer and other concerned officials to plan, discuss and identify the PPAs which need urgent implementation and prepare and submit the necessary reports for consideration of the LDRRMC; and c. require the Municipal Accountant to prepare the necessary journal entry voucher to revert back to the general fund the unutilized balances for Calendar Years 2014, 2015 and 2016 and prepare the necessary adjusting journal entries.
observations.managementAction: The LDRRMC prepared the LDRRMFIP covering current year LDRRMF and unexpended balances from the previous years; 87.72 percent of the PPAs in the LDRRMF were utilized. The unexpended balance from CYs 2014 to 2016 in the special trust fund totaling P24,465,380.03 was already used for social services projects, programs and activities in compliance with the above COA Circular.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Local Disaster Risk Reduction Management Officer in coordination with the Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator, Budget Officer and Municipal Accountant to closely monitor the implementation of the PPAs for LDRRM in the Annual Investment Plan in order to maximize the utilization of the LDRRMF and thereby improve the readiness of the Municipality in disaster prevention, mitigation and response.
observations.managementAction: Conducted
observations.reason: Only 82.00 per cent of the PPAs in the LDRRMF were utilized.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Planning and Development Officer, in coordination with the Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator, Budget Officer and Municipal Accountant, to closely monitor the implementation of projects with funding from the capital outlays for LDRRM or consider a thorough review of the unused funds to determine whether there is a need to revert and/or reprogram the funds, subject to the approval of the Sanggunian Bayan upon the Mayor’s recommendation and compliance with other pertinent rules and regulations.
observations.managementAction: Most of the PPAs were already implemented. Only 8.34 per cent of the Continuing PPAs were implemented.
observations.reason: Only 8.34 per cent of the Continuing PPAs were implemented.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Planning and Development Officer and other concerned officials to prepare a catch-up plan to ensure that the projects that had been stalled or delayed due to the pandemic are carefully implemented and if found necessary after review, the unused funds are reprogrammed for more urgent projects, subject to compliance with pertinent laws, rules and regulations thereon.
observations.managementAction: A number of PPAs were implemented. Management reported that other PPAs were also implemented and subsequently reprogrammed.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor: a. require all Inventory Custodians to ensure that all issuances of supplies and materials are covered by Requisition and Issue Slips (RIS) and the General Services Officer to use the RIS as basis for the preparation of the Summary of Supplies and Materials Issued (SSMI)
observations.managementAction: and b. instruct the General Services Officer to submit the SSMI to the Municipal Accountant together with the original copies of the RIS which will be the basis of the latter to record in the books the expended portions of supplies and materials.
observations.reason: Partially Implemented

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the immediate bonding of all Inventory Custodians in compliance with the provisions of Section 101 of P.D. No. 1445 and Section 4.2 of Treasury Circular No. 02-2009 dated August 6, 2009.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Vice Mayor was bonded and the increase of the fidelity bond of the General Services Officer was processed.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor: a. direct the Municipal Accountant to refrain from granting additional cash advances to any employee who has outstanding prior cash advance and to designate another employee as Special Disbursing Officer if it is necessary to make additional cash advances for special activities/projects
observations.managementAction: and b. direct concerned officials and employees to ensure that all cash advances granted to employees are liquidated regularly and continuously send demand letters to officers and employees who would fail to liquidate their advances on due dates with a warning that their salaries would be withheld should they still fail to liquidate after demand.
observations.reason: Municipal Accountant will withhold salaries of those who fail to liquidate.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the immediate bonding of the Municipal Vice Mayor and the increase of the fidelity bond of the General Services Officer in compliance with Section 101 of P.D. No. 1445 and Sections 4.2 and 4.3.3 of Treasury Circular No. 02-2009 dated August 6, 2009.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Vice Mayor was bonded and the increase of the fidelity bond of GSO was processed.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:

a. direct the Inventory Committee to conduct complete physical count of the
Municipality’s PPE according to their prepared Physical Inventory Plan and
prepare the corresponding Report on the Physical Count of Property, Plant and
Equipment (RPCPPE) following the guidelines set forth in COA Circular No. 2020-
006;

b. direct the General Services Officer to coordinate with the Municipal Accountant and
Municipal Engineer in securing the necessary documents like contracts and
disbursement payments of land acquisition and construction of infrastructure
assets as basis in recording and updating their respective PPE Property Cards (PC);

c. require the General Services Officer to update all PCs and records based on the
latest RPCPPE; and

d. require the General Services Officer and Municipal Accountant to exert their best
efforts to reconcile their respective PPE records to come up with the reconciled
balances of the PPE accounts to be presented in the financial statements.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant to
coordinate with the Provincial Accountant to initiate the process of reconciliation of
their respective Due from and to LGU accounts, after which make adjustments in the
books of the Municipality, if necessary.

Likewise, it is recommended that the Municipal Accountant request the Provincial
Accountant to furnish her with copies of the Journal Entry Vouchers showing the
amount collected by the Province in favor of the Municipality to serve as basis in
recording the Due from LGU in the books of the Municipality as provided under
Section 20 of the Manual on NGAS for LGUs, Volume I.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:

a. instruct the Local Disaster Risk Reduction Management Council (LDRRMC) to
facilitate the preparation of complete Local Disaster Risk Reduction Management
Fund Investment Plan (LDRRMFIP) covering current year LDRRMF and
unexpended balances from the previous years;

b. direct the Municipal Planning and Development Officer and other concerned
officials to plan, discuss and identify the DRRM related PPAs which need urgent
implementation and prepare and submit the necessary reports for consideration
of the LDRRMC; and

c. require the Municipal Accountant to prepare the necessary journal entry voucher
to revert back to the general fund the unutilized LDRRMF balances for Calendar
Years 2014, 2015 and 2016 to be used for social services projects, programs and
activities in compliance with above COA Circular.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Local Disaster Risk Reduction
Management Officer in coordination with the Municipal Planning and Development
Coordinator, Budget Officer and Municipal Accountant to closely monitor the
implementation of the PPAs for LDRRM in the Annual Investment Plan in order to
maximize the utilization of the LDRRMF and thereby improve the readiness of the
Municipality in disaster prevention, mitigation and response.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Local Disaster Risk Reduction
Management Officer, in coordination with the Municipal Planning and Development
Coordinator, Budget Officer and Municipal Accountant, to closely monitor the
implementation of projects with funding from the capital outlays for LDRRM or
consider a thorough review thereof during the budget preparation to determine
whether there is a need to revert and/or reprogram the funds, subject to the approval
of the Sanggunian Bayan upon the Mayor’s recommendation and compliance with
other pertinent rules and regulations.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Planning and
Development Coordinator and other concerned officials to prepare a catch-up plan
to ensure that the projects that had been stalled or delayed due to the pandemic are
fully implemented or if found necessary after review, the unused funds are
reprogrammed for more urgent projects, subject to compliance of the Municipality
with pertinent laws, rules and regulations thereon.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:

a. instruct all Inventory Custodians to ensure that all issuances of supplies and
materials are covered by Requisition and Issue Slips (RIS) and the General
Services Officer to use the RIS as basis for the preparation of the Summary of
Supplies and Materials Issued(SSMI); and

b. instruct the General Services Officer to submit the SSMI to the Municipal
Accountant together with the original copies of the RIS which will be the basis of
the latter to record in the books the expended portions of supplies and materials.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the immediate bonding of all
Inventory Custodians in compliance with the provisions of Section 101 of P.D.
No. 1445 and Section 4.2 of Treasury Circular No. 02-2009 dated August 6, 2009.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:

a. direct the Municipal Accountant to refrain from granting additional cash
advances to official or employee who has outstanding prior cash advance and to
designate another employee as Special Disbursing Officer if it is necessary to
make additional cash advances for special activities/projects; and

b. direct concerned officials and employees to ensure that all cash advances
granted to them are liquidated within the prescribed period. Further, it is
recommended that the Municipal Accountant regularly and continuously send
demand letters to officers and employees who would fail to liquidate their
advances on due dates with a warning that their salaries would be withheld
should they still fail to liquidate after demand.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the immediate bonding of the
Municipal Vice Mayor and the increase of the fidelity bond of the General Services
Officer in compliance with Section 101 of P.D. No. 1445 and Sections 4.2 and 4.3.3 of
Treasury Circular No. 02-2009 dated August 6, 2009.

We recommended that Management re-establish an Inventory Committee.
Inventory Committee which shall compose of adequate number of members who shall be temporarily relieved of all their regular duties to devote their full time to be able to complete the physical inventory in three months or less, in compliance with COA Circular No. 2020-006 dated January 31, 2020.
After which, it was recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the:
a. Inventory Committee to prepare a Physical Inventory Plan and accordingly conduct a comprehensive physical inventory of all PPE of the Municipality and prepare the corresponding Report on the Physical Count of Property, Plant and Equipment (RPCPPE) following the guidelines set forth in said COA Circular;
b. OIC-General Services Officer to update the GSO’s property records based on the RPCPPE; and
c. OIC-General Services Officer and OIC-Municipal Accountant to reconcile their respective property records to come up with the reconciled balances of PPE accounts to be considered as the correct balance of the PPE.
Reiterated in Part II, Observation No. 1 of this report.
observations.reason: The General Services Office was not able to finish the PPE count and was not able to complete the physical inventory.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that:

a. MSWDO provide valid justification on the deficiency noted in SAC forms with the same barcode; and

b. Municipal Accountant and MSWDO to submit to the Audit Team the lacking supporting documents.
observations.managementAction: Investigated and justified the deficiency noted in SAC forms with same barcode; and submitted the lacking supporting documents such as the certification from the concerned Barangay that the beneficiaries were in need of assistance during the pandemic, valid identification cards and photos of all beneficiaries or equivalent documents to prove receipt of their subsidy.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to strictly comply with the guidelines governing proper recording and recognition in the books of accounts of funds received from the National Government.
observations.managementAction: Recorded correctly all funds received from the National Government.

The Management committed that Inventory Custodians will ensure strict compliance with the preparation of RIS when issuing supplies and materials. Moreover, the General Services Officer will use the RIS in the preparation of the SSMI and will submit on time the original copies to the Municipal Accountant for the recognition of supplies and materials expenses.
observations.reason: The General Services Office was not yet provided the SSMI by the GSO for all inventories. Some procedures improved but not yet in all commodities/stocks.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor exercise diligence, prudence and proper control in the disposition of government funds by implementing cost-cutting measures and other stringent but necessary fiscal reforms and initiatives to significantly reduce, if not totally eliminate, the cash deficit of the Municipality.
observations.managementAction: Upon checking, the necessary cash was already transferred to P30,498,243.65 under the Trust Fund. The necessary cash was already transferred to under the Trust Fund.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor re-establish an Inventory Committee
which shall compose of adequate number of members who shall be temporarily
relieved of all their regular duties to devote their full time to be able to complete
the physical inventory in three months or less, in compliance with COA Circular
No. 2020-006 dated January 31, 2020. After which, it was recommended that the
Municipal Mayor direct the:
a. Inventory Committee to prepare a Physical Inventory Plan and accordingly
conduct a comprehensive physical inventory of all PPE of the Municipality and
prepare the corresponding Report on the Physical Count of Property, Plant
and Equipment (RPCPPE) following the guidelines set forth in said COA Circular;
b. OIC-General Services Officer to update the GSO’s property records based on
the RPCPPE; and
c. OIC-General Services Officer and OIC-Municipal Accountant to reconcile their
respective property records to come up with the reconciled balances of PPE
accounts to be considered as the correct balance of the PPE.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the:
a. MSWDO to provide valid justification on the deficiency noted in SAC forms
with the same barcode; and
b. Municipal Accountant and MSWDO to submit to the Audit Team the lacking
supporting documents such as the. certification from the concerned Barangay
that the beneficiaries were in need of assistance during the pandemic, valid
identification cards and photos of all beneficiaries or equivalent documents to
prove receipt of their subsidy.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to
strictly comply with the guidelines governing proper recording and recognition in
the books of accounts of funds received from the National Government.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the:
a. Municipal Accountant to record all purchases of supplies and materials under
the appropriate inventory account and to recognize expense only when the
items are already consumed based on the SSMI submitted by the OIC-GSO;
b. Municipal Accountant and the OIC-GSO to maintain SLC and SC, respectively,
for each commodity/stock;
c. OIC-GSO to prepare RIS for issuances of supplies/materials and consolidate
them on a weekly basis using the SSMI and furnish the Municipal Accountant
with copies thereof for recording in the books of the cost of issued items; and
d. OIC-GSO to conduct physical count of inventories semi-annually and to
prepare and submit the Report of the Physical Count of Inventories (RPCI) to
the Auditor not later than July 31 and January 31 of each year.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor and other concerned municipal
officials exercise diligence, prudence and proper control in the disposition of
government funds by implementing cost-cutting measures and other stringent but
necessary fiscal reforms and initiatives to significantly reduce, if not totally
eliminate, the cash deficit of the Municipality.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the:
a. Inventory Committee to conduct a complete physical count of all PPE of the Municipality and the corresponding Report on the Physical Count of Property, Plant and Equipment (RPCPPE) be submitted to the Office of the Auditor on or before January 31 of each year; and
b. OIC-Municipal Accountant and OIC-General Services Officer to:
• analyze and reconcile the discrepancies in the balances of some PPE accounts and make the necessary adjustments; and
• update PPELCs, RPLCs and Property Cards, and ensure that necessary information for proper monitoring and control of the assets of the Municipality are completely recorded therein.
observations.managementAction: The OIC-Municipal Accountant and OIC-General Services Officer started the reconciliation of records.
observations.reason: The Municipality was not able to conduct their physical inventory of the PPE.

observations.recommendation:
We reiterated our prior year’s recommendations that the Municipal Mayor direct the OIC-GSO to:
a. Prepare the required Inventory and Inspection Report of Unserviceable Property (IIRUP) for all the unserviceable motor vehicles and file an application for disposal duly supported with all the required documents to the Office of the COA Auditor;
b. Prepare and/or update the existing Acknowledgement Receipts for Equipment (ARE) for all motor vehicles enumerated in Appendix 6 to establish and monitor accountability over their use; and
c. Ensure that disposal of unserviceable properties is in accordance with the procedures and guidelines provided under Section 79 of PD 1445 and other existing laws, rules and regulations on disposal.
observations.managementAction: The motor vehicles were already disposed.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Planning and Development Officer and other concerned officials to ensure that proper planning was conducted and all issues related to project implementation are resolved before allocating funds therefor to minimize the risk of non-delivery of the projects.
observations.managementAction: Proper planning was conducted.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the MDRRMO to:
a. ensure that all activities on programs, projects and activities on disaster prevention, mitigation and preparedness included the MDRRM Plan are implemented accordingly; and
b. provide specific information on the timetables or schedules of implementation of the projects and activities in the MDRRM Plan for proper guidance and monitoring of their implementation.
observations.managementAction: The MDRRMO ensured that all programs, projects and activities on disaster prevention, mitigation and preparedness included the MDRRM Plan are implemented accordingly; and provided specific information on the timetables or schedules of implementation of the projects and activities in the MDRRM Plan for proper guidance and monitoring of their implementation.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Head of Municipal Planning and Development Office to establish and maintain a GAD Database containing statistics and sex disaggregated data systematically gathered and regularly updated to serve as basis for gender-responsive planning, programming and policy formulation and subjected to gender analysis, in compliance with Section 4.B, Item 1 of Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2013-01 of PCW, DILG, DBM and NEDA. We also recommended that the Municipality continue with its effort to capacitate the personnel involved in the GAD Program by sending them to attend relevant trainings and workshops.
observations.managementAction: A GAD Database was established.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the:
a. Inventory Committee to conduct a complete physical count of all PPE of the Municipality
to determine their actual existence, and the corresponding Report on the Physical Count
of Property, Plant and Equipment (RPCPPE) be submitted to the Office of the Auditor on
or before January 31 of each year;
b. OIC-Municipal Accountant and OIC-General Services Officer to:
• analyze and reconcile the discrepancies in the balances of some PPE accounts
between their records and make the necessary adjustments; and
• update PPELCs, RPLCs and Property Cards, and ensure that necessary information
for proper monitoring and control of the assets of the Municipality are completely
recorded therein.

We reiterated our prior year’s recommendations that the Municipal Mayor direct the
OIC-GSO to:
a. Prepare the required Inventory and Inspection Report of Unserviceable Property
(IIRUP) for all the unserviceable motor vehicles and file an application for
disposal duly supported with all the required documents to the Office of the COA
Auditor;
b. Prepare and/or update the existing Acknowledgement Receipts for Equipment
(ARE) for all motor vehicles enumerated in Appendix 6 to establish and monitor
accountability over their use; and
c. Ensure that disposal of unserviceable properties is in accordance with the
procedures and guidelines provided under Section 79 of PD 1445 and other
existing laws, rules and regulations on disposal.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Planning and
Development Officer and other concerned officials to ensure that proper planning
was conducted and all issues related to project implementation are resolved before
allocating funds therefor to minimize the risk of non-delivery of the projects.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the MDRRMO to:
a. ensure that all programs, projects and activities on disaster prevention,
mitigation and preparedness included the MDRRM Plan are implemented
accordingly; and
b. provide specific information on the timetables or schedules of implementation
of the projects and activities in the MDRRM Plan for proper guidance and
monitoring of their implementation.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Head of Municipal Planning
and Development Office to establish and maintain a GAD Database containing
statistics and sex disaggregated data systematically gathered and regularly
updated to serve as basis for gender-responsive planning, programming and policy
formulation and subjected to gender analysis, in compliance with Section 4.B, Item
1 of Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2013-01 of PCW, DILG, DBM and NEDA.

We also recommended that the Municipality continue with its effort to capacitate
the personnel involved in the GAD Program by sending them to attend relevant
trainings and workshops.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the: a. Inventory Committee to conduct a complete physical count of all the property and equipment of the Municipality to determine/confirm its actual condition and existence, and the corresponding Report on the Physical Count of Property and Equipment (RPCPPE) be submitted to the Office of the Auditor on or before January 31 of each year; b. Municipal Accountant and OIC-General Services Officer to prepare, maintain and update the PPELCs, RPLCs and Property Cards and ensure that necessary information for proper recording, monitoring and control of the assets of the Municipality are completely recorded therein; and c. Municipal Accountant and OIC-General Services Officer to immediately reconcile the result of every physical count with the accounting records and prepare the adjustments where needed.
observations.managementAction: The Inventory Committee conducted a complete physical count of the property and Municipality. The Report on the Physical Count of the property and Municipality. submitted to the Office of the Auditor on or before January 31 of each year; The Municipal Accountant and OIC-General Services Officer started preparing and updating the PPELCs, RPLCs and Property Cards. As of March 2018 the PPELCs, RPLCs and Property Cards were incomplete/un-updated. The OIC-GSO started the reconciliation of records with accounting department. Part II, Observation No. 1 of this report.
observations.reason: The Report on the Physical Count of Property and Equipment (RPCPPE) was submitted to the Office of the Auditor only in June 2020. Updating of PPELCs, RPLCs and Property Cards were started but not yet complete. Reconciliation of records with accounting department not yet completed.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor: a. Direct the Municipal Treasurer to furnish the Municipal Accounting Office with the duly certified list showing the names of taxpayers and the amount due and collectible for the year to enable the latter to set up the RPT and SET Receivables for the current year correctly; and b. Direct the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer to analyze and investigate the prior year entries and make the necessary adjustments in their records and henceforth ensure that the two accounts have equal balances at anytime of the year.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Treasurer furnished the Municipal accounting department with the duly certified list of taxpayers and the amount due and collectible for the year. Adjustments were made after thorough analysis of the prior entries made.
observations.reason: Adjustments were made. There were no more discrepancies between balances of the RPT and SET Receivables and the List of Unpaid Taxes as of December 31, 2019.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor: a. Require the Municipal Accountant to ensure that all lawful and valid expenditures and obligations incurred during the year are taken-up in the books of that year for proper presentation of accounts in the financial statements; b. Instruct the Municipal Treasurer to send notice to the payee of the check one month prior to the date when the check shall become stale pursuant to Section 59 of the Manual on the New Government Accounting System (MNGAS), Volume I; and c. Advise the Municipal Accountant to prepare the necessary journal entries to record the bank reconciling items totaling P220,383.67.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accountant ensured that all lawful and valid expenditures and obligations incurred during the year were taken-up in the books. Notices were sent to the payees of the checks one month prior to the date when checks issued shall become stale pursuant to Section 59 of MNGAS. Journal Entries have been prepared to record the bank reconciling items.
observations.reason: As of to date, the 52 disbursement vouchers were posted and recorded already. Notices were sent to payees. Journal entries have been prepared to record the bank reconciling items totaling P220,383.67.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant to take up in the books of accounts under the Receivable and the corresponding Income accounts the unpaid stall rentals and utilities of Silang (Poblacion) Public Market and Bulihan Public Market as of December 31, 2018 amounting to P5,503,751.36 and P2,715,372.28, respectively, and likewise instruct the Municipal Treasurer to prepare and submit to the Municipal Accountant on a monthly basis reports of collections and receivables pertaining to market operations for proper recording thereof.
observations.managementAction: The unpaid stall rentals and utilities of Silang (Poblacion) Public Market and Bulihan Public Market as of December 31, 2018 were already taken up in the books.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor: a. Require the concerned cash officers/employees to immediately liquidate their outstanding cash advances and instruct the Municipal Accountant to ensure that all cash advances shall be settled at year-end pursuant to the above-cited existing regulation; b. Instruct the Municipal Accountant to re-classify the erroneous entries in the Advances to Officers and Employees of P5,372,197.35 and P2,448,600.00 to Due from Officers and Employees and the Advances to Special Disbursing Officers, respectively. Henceforth, classify cash advances according to its purpose/nature and record the same using the appropriate account prescribed in RCALGU to ensure correctness in the balances of the accounts in the financial statements; and c. Take appropriate action to effect the settlement of the cash advances of employees who were either terminated or deceased amounting to P497,230.00.
observations.managementAction: Outstanding cash advances were already liquidated as of August 2019. Erroneous entries were already re-classified. All coop loans were reconciled and necessary adjusting entries made. Demand Letters were sent to the terminated employees/deceased estates for the settlement of the cash advances.
observations.reason: The outstanding advances were liquidated; erroneous entries were reclassified. Demand letters were sent to the terminated/ deceased employees; the total amount of P497,230.00 was not liquidated as of December 31, 2019.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer remit the shares of the barangays from real property taxes within the prescribed period within which to do so in order for them to utilize the said funds for their programs and projects and be able to deliver the basic services to their constituents. We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant further review and analyze the balances of the Due to LGUs account for barangays and if found to be valid, remit the same to the concerned agency. Adjusting entries should be made where appropriate to reflect accurate balances of accounts in the books.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Treasurer remitted the shares of the barangays from real property taxes. Adjustments were made to reflect accurate balances of accounts in the books.

observations.recommendation: We reiterated our prior year’s recommendations that the Municipal Mayor adhere to the provision of Section 5 of DILG-DBM Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2017-1 to ensure the optimal utilization of 20% DF and achieve the desirable socio-economic development and environmental management outcomes of the municipality.
observations.managementAction: Silang bears the responsibility in the implementatio n of the identified 5 projects included in the 20 per cent Development Fund for 2018. The Rehabilitation/ Improvement of Public Rest Area of PNPA is still under negotiations since the LGU is still waiting for the necessary documents in order for them to commence the project from the Department of Interior and Local Government.
observations.reason: Some projects have not yet commenced.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the: a. BPLO, in coordination with the Municipal Treasurer, to use the list of payments made to contractors/suppliers who transacted with the Municipality of Silang in the preceding calendar year to validate their declared sales from the same transactions; b. Municipal Treasurer to require every contractor/supplier to submit Sworn Statement of Gross Receipt or Sales of the preceding year at the time of payment of business tax; and c. Enforce the collection of under-payment of business tax of P34,947.60 in CY 2018 including penalties and surcharges, if applicable, pursuant to the rates provided in the Municipal Revenue Code.
observations.managementAction: BPLO, in coordination with the Municipal Treasurer, gathered information from the list of payments made to contractors/suppliers. The Municipal Treasurer sent letters of collection to five businesses and required them to bring sworn statement of gross receipts or sales of the preceding year at the time of payment of business tax to validate their declared sales from the same transactions. The treasurer’s office enforced payment of under declaration of receipts.

observations.recommendation: We reiterated our prior year’s recommendations that the Municipal Mayor direct the OIC-GSO to: a. Prepare the required Inventory and Inspection Report of Unserviceable Property (IIRUP) for all the unserviceable motor vehicles and file an application for disposal duly supported with all the required documents to the Office of the COA Auditor; b. Prepare and/or update the existing Acknowledgement Receipts for Equipment (ARE) for all motor vehicles enumerated in Appendix 13; and c. Ensure that the disposal of unserviceable properties undergo proper disposal procedures following the existing relevant guidelines on the disposal of government properties.
observations.managementAction: The OIC-GSO prepared the Report of Unserviceable Property and Inventory and Inspection (IIRUP) for the unserviceable motor vehicles. Acknowledgement Receipts for Equipment (ARE) for vehicles have been updated.
observations.reason: Application for disposal with all the required documents was not filed to the Office of the COA Auditor. Not all Acknowledgement Receipts for Equipment (ARE) for vehicles have been updated. There was still no disposal of properties as of December 31, 2019.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor: a. Instruct the Municipal Accountant to draw an entry to re-classify the grants extended to farmers and various cooperatives to Grants and Donation account, except the Quedancor litigation account; and b. Direct the Municipal Accountant, Municipal Coop Development Officer and the Municipal Social Welfare and Development Office to exert efforts to reconcile the differences of P245,777.97 and P750,444.45 between their records to establish the correct amount of the Loans Receivable – Others account.
observations.managementAction: Dormant accounts were closed to Grants and donation account, except the Quedancor litigation. All coop loans were reconciled and necessary adjusting entries were made. The Municipal Coop Development Officer and the Municipal Social Welfare and Development Office reconciled the differences.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
a. Request for authority to write-off dormant receivable accounts aged ten (10) years and above from the Commission on Audit supported by the relevant documents provided in Section 8.3 of the COA Circular No. 2016-005 dated December 19, 2016;
b. Instruct the Municipal Coop Development Officer to continue establishing the existence/validity of the remaining loans receivable accounts, review the collectability of these receivables, demand payments from beneficiaries who have the ability to pay and exhaust all available remedies to enforce collection; and
c. Direct the Municipal Accountant to reclassify the recorded Advances to Receivables – Disallowance/Charges of account in CY P2,059,698.22 to Advances to Officers and Employees account and facilitate the preparation for a request from the Commission on Audit to write off such accounts.
observations.managementAction:
Request for write-off is still on-going, no request was submitted to Office of Auditor.
These amounts still existed in the receivables records.
observations.reason:
Request for write-off is still on-going, no request was submitted to Office of Auditor.
These amounts still existed in the receivables records.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
a. Require the concerned officers/employees to immediately liquidate their outstanding cash advances and instruct the Municipal Accountant to ensure that all cash advances shall be settled at year-end pursuant to the above-cited existing regulation.
b. Take appropriate action to effect the settlement of the cash advances of persons who were either terminated or deceased amounting to P497,230.00; and
c. Instruct the Municipal Accountant to withhold the salaries of the present Accountable Officers who failed to liquidate their long outstanding cash advances.
observations.managementAction:
The Municipality still outstanding cash advances at year-end.
Liquidations were not made on time.
observations.reason:
The Municipality still outstanding cash advances at year-end.
Liquidations were not made on time.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the:

a. Inventory Committee to conduct a complete physical count of all the property and
equipment of the Municipality to determine/confirm its actual condition and
existence, and the corresponding Report on the Physical Count of Property, Plant
and Equipment (RPCPPE) be submitted to the Office of the Auditor on or before
January 31 of each year;
b. Municipal Accountant and OIC-General Services Officer to prepare, maintain and
update the PPELCs, RPLCs and Property Cards, and ensure their completeness as
to the necessary information for proper recording, monitoring and control of the
assets of the Municipality; and
c. Municipal Accountant and OIC-General Services Officer to immediately reconcile
the result of every physical count with the property and accounting records and
prepare the adjustments where needed.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:

a. Direct the Municipal Treasurer to furnish the Municipal Accounting Office with the
duly certified list showing the names of taxpayers and the amount due and
collectible for the year 2019 to enable the latter Office to set up the RPT and SET
Receivables for the current year correctly; and
b. Direct the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer to analyze and
investigate the cause/s of the net deficiencies of P139,432,375.89 between balances
of the RPT and SET Receivables and the List of Unpaid Taxes as of December 31,
2018, make the necessary adjustments in their records and henceforth ensure that
the two accounts have equal balances at anytime of the year.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:

a. Require the Municipal Accountant to ensure that all lawful and valid expenditures and
obligations incurred during the year are taken-up in the books of that year for proper
presentation of accounts in the financial statements;
b. Instruct the Municipal Treasurer to send notice to the payee of the check one month
prior to the date when check issued shall become stale pursuant to Section 59 of
the Manual on the New Government Accounting System (MNGAS), Volume I; and
c. Advise the Municipal Accountant to prepare the necessary journal entries to record
the bank reconciling items totaling P220,383.67.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant to take
up in the books of accounts under the Receivable and the corresponding Income
accounts covering the unpaid stall rentals and utilities of Silang (Poblacion) Public
Market and Bulihan Public Market as of December 31, 2018 amounting to P5,503,751.36
and P2,715,372.28, respectively, and likewise instruct the Municipal Treasurer to
prepare and submit to the Municipal Accountant on a monthly basis reports of
collections and receivables pertaining to market operations for proper recording
thereof.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:

a. Require the concerned officers/employees to immediately liquidate their
outstanding cash advances and instruct the Municipal Accountant to ensure that
all cash advances shall be settled at year-end pursuant to the above-cited existing
regulation.
b. Instruct the Municipal Accountant to re-classify the erroneous entries in the
Advances to Officers and Employees of P5,372,197.35 and P2,448,600.00 to Due
from Officers and Employees and the Advances to Special Disbursing Officers
accounts, respectively. Henceforth, classify cash advances according to its
purpose/nature and record the same using the appropriate account prescribed in
RCALGU to ensure correctness in the balances of the accounts in the financial
statements; and
c. Take appropriate action to effect the settlement of the cash advances of persons
who were either terminated or deceased amounting to P497,230.00.

We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer remit the shares of the barangays from
real property taxes within the prescribed period within which to do so in order for them to
utilize the said funds for their programs and projects and be able to deliver the basic
services to their constituents.

We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant further review and analyze the
balances of the Due to LGUs account for barangays and if found to be valid, remit the
same to the concerned agency. Adjusting entries should be made where appropriate to
reflect accurate balances of accounts in the books as well as in the financial statements.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor adhere to the provision of Section 5 of
DILG-DBM Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2017-1 to ensure the optimal utilization of
20% DF and fully achieve the desirable socio-economic development and
environmental management outcomes of the municipality.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the:

a. BPLO, in coordination with the Municipal Treasurer, to use the information gathered
from the list of payments made to contractors/suppliers who transacted with the
Municipality of Silang in the preceding calendar year to validate their declared sales
from the same transactions;
b. Municipal Treasurer to require every contractor/supplier to submit Sworn Statement
of Gross Receipt or Sales of the preceding year at the time of payment of business
tax; and
c. Enforce the collection of under-payment of business tax of P34,947.60 in CY 2018
including penalties and surcharges, if applicable, pursuant to the rates provided in the
Municipal Revenue Code.

We reiterated our prior year’s recommendations that the Municipal Mayor:

a. Direct the OIC-GSO to:
a.1 Prepare the required Inventory and Inspection Report of Unserviceable
Property (IIRUP) for all the unserviceable motor vehicles and file an application
for disposal duly supported with all the required documents to the Office of
the COA Auditor; and
a.2 Prepare and/or update the existing Acknowledgement Receipts for Equipment
(ARE) for all motor vehicles enumerated in Appendix 13.
b. Ensure that the unserviceable properties undergo proper disposal procedures
following the existing relevant guidelines on the disposal of government
properties.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:

a. Instruct the Municipal Accountant to draw an entry to re-classify the grants
extended to farmers and to various cooperatives totaling P5,493,911.12; and
b. Direct the Municipal Accountant, Municipal Coop Development Officer and the
Municipal Social Welfare and Development Office to exert efforts to reconcile the
differences of P245,777.97 and P750,444.45 between their records to establish the
correct amount to of the Loans Receivable – Others account.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
a. Create an Inventory Committee to yearly conduct complete physical inventory of all assets to determine its actual existence;
b. Municipal Accountant and the OIC-GSO to analyze and reconcile the various calendar year discrepancies in balances of PPE and inventory reports and make the necessary accounting adjustments and regularly reconcile the result of the physical count with the property and accounting records; and
c. Municipal Accountant and the OIC-GSO to prepare, update and complete PPELCs, RPLC and records of all property items by providing necessary information on both records to facilitate verification and reconciliation.
observations.managementAction:
The Municipal Management created an Inventory Committee.
The Municipal Accountant and the GSO was not able to update and complete PPELCs, RPLC and property records of all property items.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
a. Direct the Municipal Accountant to initially record all disbursement vouchers by crediting the Accounts Payable and upon release of check, post the payment by debiting the same account and crediting the Cash in Bank account for proper presentation of accounts in the financial statements.
b. Instruct the Municipal Treasurer to send notice to the payee of the check one (1) month prior to the date when check issued shall become stale pursuant to Section 59 of the Manual on the New Government Accounting System (MNGAS), Volume I; and
c. Advise the Municipal Accountant to prepare the necessary journal entries to record the cancellation of the above-mentioned checks using the pro-forma accounting entries prescribed under Section 61 (6) of the same manual.
observations.managementAction:
There were still disbursement vouchers which were not recorded at year-end.
The Municipal Treasurer sent notices to the payees of the check which turned stale. Adjusting entries were prepared by the Municipal Accountant.
observations.reason: There were still disbursement vouchers which were not recorded at year-end.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant to take up in the books of accounts under the Receivable and the corresponding Income accounts covering the unpaid stall rentals and utilities of Silang (Poblacion) Public Market and Bulihan Public Market as of December 31, 2017 amounting to P3,235,418.54 and P13,487,397.29, respectively.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accountant recorded the receivables for CY 2018.
observations.reason: Receivables from market operations for CY 2018 were recorded. The receivables for CY 2017 were not recorded because no report was submitted to the accounting office as basis of recording.

observations.recommendation:
We reiterated our prior year’s recommendation that the Municipal Mayor instruct the OIC-GSO to:
a. Prepare the required Inventory and Inspection Report of Unserviceable Property (IIRUP) for all the unserviceable motor vehicles and file an application for disposal with appropriate documents to the Office of the COA Auditor; and
b. Reconcile the actual count of unserviceable properties between the CY 2017 report and the prior year’s report of unserviceable properties.
observations.managementAction:
The GSO was not able to identify specific items to be disposed due to lack of records.
The GSO was not able to reconcile the discrepancy between the CY 2017 report and the prior year report of unserviceable properties.
observations.reason: The GSO was not able to identify specific items to be disposed due to lack of records.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Local Development Council (LDC) to ensure that only the programs and expenses which were geared towards the attainment of socio-economic development and that partake the nature of investment or capital expenditures will be given allocation under the 20% Development Fund to ensure full realization of the purpose for which the DF was established.
observations.managementAction: The Municipality refrained from charging the 20% DF expenses which were not related to the nature of the fund.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
a. Observe strict adherence to paragraph 6.3 of NDRRMC-DBM-DILG Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2013-1 to ensure proper use of the LDRRM Funds;
b. Instruct the Municipal Disaster Risk Reduction Management Officer (MDRRMO) and Municipal Social Welfare Development Officer (MSWDO) to provide the Distribution List of the above subject purchases duly acknowledged by beneficiaries/recipients and indicating therein the quantity and kind of welfare goods received in order to account the full liquidation of the subject purchases and to attest to the veracity of the purchases. Henceforth, all assistance or goods distributed to intended beneficiaries be properly documented to ensure that the resources of the government are managed and utilized in accordance with the established rules and regulations; and
c. Direct the Municipal Accountant to comply with the accounting and reporting guidelines on the utilization of the LDRRMF prescribed under COA Circular Nos. 2014-002 and 2015-009 dated April 15, 2014 and December 1, 2015, respectively, to ensure the correctness and reliability of the financial reporting of the Municipality.
observations.managementAction:
The MDRRMO made sure that they comply with the proper use of the LDRRM Funds.
Proper documentation was observed to avoid the incurrence of the same.
Proper documentation was observed.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Public Health Worker to refund the excess amount of hazard pay received. We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the three (3) Social Welfare Officers to refund the overpayment of hazard pay totaling P57,099.31. Henceforth, take into consideration the leaves of absence incurred in the computation of hazard pay as prescribed by DBM-DOH Joint Circular No. 1 s. 2016 and compute the said benefit using the prescribed formula provided in paragraph 16.1.3.2 of the IRR of RA No. 9433.
observations.managementAction:
Refund was made for the excess amount of hazard pay received.
Refund was made for the excess amount of hazard pay received.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
a. Direct the OIC-GSO to:
a.1 Exert efforts in locating the 17 missing and/or stolen motor vehicles of the Municipality and make proper actions;
a.2 Coordinate with the concerned persons to acquire the original copies of the OR/CR of the 14 motor vehicles and the Municipal Accountant to make sure that succeeding purchases of motor vehicles were supported with complete documentations;
a.3 Prepare the required Inventory and Inspection Report of Unserviceable Property (IIRUP) for all the unserviceable motor vehicles amounting to P12,132,700.00 of the Municipality and file an application for disposal with appropriate documents to the Office of the COA Auditor; and
a.4 Prepare and/or update the Acknowledgement Receipt for Equipment (ARE) for all motor vehicles enumerated in Annex 11.
b. Cause the remittance of the P65,000.00 from the sale of two motor vehicles to the Municipal Treasurer and the latter to issue an official receipt on the said amount; and
c. Ensure that disposal of unserviceable properties should undergo proper disposal procedures and properly documented following the existing relevant guidelines on the disposal of government properties.
observations.managementAction:
Succeeding purchases of motor vehicles were supported with complete documentation.
IIRUP for some of the items to be submitted to the Audit Team.
The amount was remitted to the Municipal Treasurer. AREs for motor vehicles were not prepared and/or updated because the inventory committee did not conduct complete physical count of its PPE.
Documents for the disposal of unserviceable motor vehicles were not yet prepared and submitted to the Audit Team.
observations.reason:
Ten motor vehicles were still missing.
The remaining seven motor vehicles were still missing.
Original copies of OR/CR remained missing.
The Inventory Committee did not conduct complete physical count of its PPE.
Documents for the disposal of unserviceable motor vehicles were not yet prepared and submitted to the Audit Team.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor make official communication with Lexsys Technologies Inc. to address the defects noted on the systems it developed and if disregarded take appropriate legal action in so that the Municipality will be properly indemnified.
observations.managementAction: The Municipality made official communication with Lexsys Technologies to address the issues and concerns.

¢. Municpal Accountant and the OIC-GSO to prepare, update and
